name: Wormwood
description: Wormwood (Artemisia absinthium) holds a significant place in European mythology and folklore, steeped in associations with bitterness, protection, and the spirit world. Its intensely bitter taste, derived from the compound thujone, contributed to its reputation as a potent medicinal herb and a key ingredient in the infamous spirit absinthe. Across various European traditions, wormwood was employed as a ward against evil spirits, often hung above doorways or burned as incense to purify spaces. It was believed to possess the power to induce visions and enhance psychic abilities, making it a favored herb among witches and healers. The plant's connection to the moon goddess Artemis (in Greek mythology, from whom its genus name 'Artemisia' is derived) further cemented its association with feminine power, intuition, and the hidden realms. Wormwood's use in absinthe, popularized in 19th-century France, led to its association with artistic inspiration, madness, and altered states of consciousness. Despite its controversial history and the restrictions placed on absinthe due to concerns about thujone's psychoactive effects, wormwood continues to be revered in some circles for its medicinal and spiritual properties. It is viewed as a powerful protector, a gateway to deeper understanding, and a symbol of both bitterness and healing.
subtitle: Bitterness, Protection, and Visionary Herb

keyMyths.title: Wormwood and Absinthe
keyMyths.description: Absinthe, flavored with wormwood, became associated with artists and writers in 19th-century France, believed to inspire creativity but also linked to madness and addiction. The drink's reputation contributed to wormwood's mystique and its portrayal as a powerful, potentially dangerous herb.
keyMyths.source: Various historical accounts and literature from the 19th and early 20th centuries

sources.author: Cunningham, Scott
sources.description: Provides information on the magical properties and uses of wormwood in various traditions.
